---
title: "US State Clustering Analysis"
author: "Roger Blake"
format: revealjs
editor: visual
---

## Setup {visibility="hidden"}

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Load libraries
library(factoextra)
library(knitr)

# 1. Define variables and exclude AK/HI
vars_to_keep <- c("Income", "Illiteracy", "Life Exp", "Murder", "HS Grad", "Frost", "Area")
exclude_states <- c("Alaska", "Hawaii")

# 2. Create logical index for the lower 48 states
keep_idx <- !(rownames(state.x77) %in% exclude_states)

# 3. Subset the dataframe to the lower 48
df_lower48 <- as.data.frame(state.x77)[keep_idx, vars_to_keep]

# 4. CRITICAL: Subset state.center coordinates to match the 48 states
df_lower48$Longitude <- state.center$x[keep_idx]
df_lower48$Latitude <- state.center$y[keep_idx]

# 5. Scale the final geo-aware dataset
df_geo_scaled <- scale(df_lower48)
```
## Introduction

What regions make up the contiguous United States?

+ The Census defines 4 regions and 9 divisions. Are these accurate?

Methodology: 

+ Use k-means clustering to cluster similar states together
+ Once clusters are established, further statistical testing can be done
+ Variables to work with (from state dataset): Income, Illiteracy, Life Expectancy, Murder, HS Grad, Frost, Area, Coordinates of the center

## Following Census Regions (k=4) {.scrollable}
:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r}
# Run k-means for k=4 with spatial awareness
set.seed(428)
km_geo_4 <- kmeans(df_geo_scaled, centers = 4, nstart = 25)

# Append results back to main dataframe
df_lower48$Geo_Cluster_4 <- as.factor(km_geo_4$cluster)

# Visualize Spatially-Aware k=4
fviz_cluster(km_geo_4, data = df_geo_scaled, main = "Geo-Weighted K-Means (k=4, Lower 48)")
```
:::

::: {.column width="35%" style="font-size: 0.55em;"}
```{r}
# Create mapping and aggregate into a comma-separated list
state_map_geo_4 <- data.frame(State = rownames(df_lower48), Cluster = df_lower48$Geo_Cluster_4)
table_geo_4 <- aggregate(State ~ Cluster, data = state_map_geo_4, FUN = function(x) paste(x, collapse = ", "))

# Print clean table
kable(table_geo_4, col.names = c("Cluster", "States Included"))
```
:::
::::
## Comparison with Census Regions {.scrollable}

![Census.gov](C:/Users/roger/STAT3675/Project/STAT3675_finalProject/Screenshot 2026-04-28 002725.png)
Takeaways:

+ New "Well-off Sun Belt" region appears
+ South (Cluster 3) remains extremely similar, minus "Well-off Sun Belt" states
+ Clusters 2 and 4 split the remaining states roughly along east/west lines


## Following Census Divisions (k=9) {.scrollable}
:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r}
# Run k-means for k=9 with spatial awareness
set.seed(123)
km_geo_9 <- kmeans(df_geo_scaled, centers = 9, nstart = 25)

# Append results back to main dataframe
df_lower48$Geo_Cluster_9 <- as.factor(km_geo_9$cluster)

# Visualize Spatially-Aware k=9
fviz_cluster(km_geo_9, data = df_geo_scaled, main = "Geo-Weighted K-Means (k=9, Lower 48)")
```
:::

::: {.column width="35%" style="font-size: 0.55em;"}
```{r}
# Create mapping and aggregate into a comma-separated list
state_map_geo_9 <- data.frame(State = rownames(df_lower48), Cluster = df_lower48$Geo_Cluster_9)
table_geo_9 <- aggregate(State ~ Cluster, data = state_map_geo_9, FUN = function(x) paste(x, collapse = ", "))

# Print clean table
kable(table_geo_9, col.names = c("Cluster", "States Included"))
```
:::
::::

## Comparison with Census Divisions {.scrollable}
![Census.gov](C:/Users/roger/STAT3675/Project/STAT3675_finalProject/Screenshot 2026-04-28 002725.png)

+ South region is split along north/south lines instead of east/west
+ Texas gets its own region
+ New England stays perfectly intact
+ Florida is again grouped with Western Sun Belt states

## Conclusions

+ Midwestern states of MI, OH, IL, and IN were constantly grouped with Northeastern states of NJ, NY and PA. Perhaps these regions are more similar than people realize. 
+ Florida seems more similar to its Southwestern sun belt companions than its neighbors in the Southeast.
+ Texas, Florida, and California were very difficult to cluster; perhaps manually assigning them a region would be the best course of action.
+ In my paper, I will delve more into statistical comparisons between regions
